name: The Moirai (Fates)
shortDescription: The Moirai, also known as the Fates, were the personifications of destiny in Greek mythology. They controlled the thread of life of every mortal and immortal, from birth to death.
description: The Moirai, or Fates, were three goddesses who determined the course of human life in Greek mythology. They were Clotho (the Spinner), who spun the thread of life; Lachesis (the Allotter), who measured the length of the thread; and Atropos (the Inflexible), who cut the thread at the end of life. Their decisions were absolute and irreversible, even for the gods. While accounts of their parentage vary, they are commonly described as daughters of Nyx (Night) or Zeus and Themis. They were depicted as old women, often spinning or holding shears, and their power extended over all living beings, dictating the length and quality of their lives. They were seen as impartial and just, ensuring that everyone received their due portion of suffering and happiness. The Moirai were crucial figures representing the inevitability of fate and the limitations of free will, serving as a constant reminder of the power of destiny in human affairs. Their influence permeated all aspects of Greek life, from birth rituals to funeral ceremonies, and they were often invoked in prayers and oaths. The Moirai were feared and respected for their unyielding authority and their role in maintaining the cosmic order. Their association with death is significant, linking them to the natural end of life's journey and reinforcing the acceptance of mortality as an intrinsic part of existence. Some stories suggest even Zeus himself was subject to their decrees.
